stem: A hospital uses a serum ferritin test to screen for iron deficiency anemia. The test has a sensitivity of 90% and a specificity of 82%. The prevalence of iron deficiency anemia in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.2% and 3.6%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has iron deficiency anemia? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(iron deficiency anemia | positive test) ranges from 0.101 to 0.157 as prevalence varies from 2.2% to 3.6%. Point estimate at 2.9% prevalence: 0.13.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.18.
The confidence interval [0.101, 0.157] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (90%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
